title: Fragaria eflagellis
intro:
There is limited information on Fragaria eflagellis, so I will provide information on a similar or closely related species, Fragaria vesca (Wood Strawberry).

Fragaria vesca, commonly known as the Wood Strawberry or Wild Strawberry, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Rosaceae family. The Wood Strawberry has been traditionally used in folk medicine due to its potential health benefits, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, contributing to its use in treating various health conditions such as wounds, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Fragaria vesca include flavonoids, ellagic acid, and anthocyanins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Fragaria vesca include teas, infusions, tinctures, and dried extracts, which are used to support health and wellness.

Fragaria species, including strawberries, have been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The leaves and roots of some Fragaria species contain anthocyanins, which have been reported to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In some cultures, Fragaria is used to treat digestive issues and to reduce inflammation. The seeds of Fragaria contain fragarine, which has been used to treat fever and other conditions. side_effects: Fragaria eflagellis, also known as the woodland strawberry, is not commonly reported to have any significant side effects when consumed in moderation. However, eating large quantities of the plant's berries or leaves may cause stomach upset or allergic reactions in some individuals. The plant's saponins, a type of natural compound found in its leaves and stems, may also cause skin irritation in people with sensitive skin. In rare cases, Fragaria eflagellis may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, due to its high vitamin K content. More research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Fragaria eflagellis.
